The chief complaint is palpitations MEDICAL NECESSITY: This visit with a FTF encounter with the patient in the home is not a visit of convenience. This home visit is necessary to prevent exacerbations of patient's ongoing medical condition. Patient is homebound, ambulates with hemicane, with poor endurance and balance, unsteady gait, generalized muscle weakness, fatigues easily, dyspnea with moderate exertion and consequently leaving home would require a considerable and taxing effort. It is safer for patient to be seen in their own environment in lieu of an office setting. Subjective Patient, Robert Bryant is an 71 year-old African American male, alert and oriented x4. He lives with roommates, in a 2 story home. Patient is being seen today with complaint of palpitations. Patient reports noticing palpitations the last few weeks and he correlates it when he is taking the nifedipine. Patient stopped nifedipine and palpitations would go away. He is wondering if its related. Patient instructed that nifedipine can cause reflex tachycardia. He denies any chest pain or shortness of breath. He has no blood pressure machine to check his vitals, will attempts to get him one through his medicaid. Patient established with RTC, but will hold off on outpatient therapy till we settle on the palpitations. Will order chest x-ray and ekg, will discontinue nifedipine for now. Skilled nursing is needed for monitoring vital signs, teachings on disease processes, home safety, education on medication use and side effects. With patient not being able to attend outpatient therapy, patient will benefit with occupational therapy to continue working on upper body strengthening and conditioning. Patient has PMHx CVA with right-sided hemiparesis, constipation, bilateral testicular microlithiasis, and multiple left epididymal cysts. Surgical Hx right inguinal hernia repair (11/2023) This visit was conducted following strict infection control measures as per CDC guidelines. Patient is homebound due to poor endurance and balance, unsteady gait, weakness, fatigues easily, dyspnea with moderate exertion and requires a taxing effort to leave home. Uses motorized wheelchair for long distances ROS: All body systems reviewed Constitutional: No significant changes in weight. Denies fever, chills. HEENT: Denies eye pain. Reports blurred vision. Denies hearing problem. No odynophagia or dysphagia. No lumps in neck. No epistaxis. No hemoptysis. Cardiac: Denies chest pain. Denies swelling. Reports palpitations after taking nifedipine Respiratory: Denies hemoptysis, wheeze. Reports short of breath with moderate exertion. GI: Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. Long-term care hospital (LTCH) NA Patient was not discharged from an inpatient facility (M1005) Inpatient Discharge Date (Most Recent): mm/dd/yyyy UK Unknown Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element): Robert Bryant, a 71-year-old male patient admitted to home health care because they have significant functional impairments and medical conditions that severely limit their ability to leave the home without assistance. The patient is considered homebound as the patient ambulates with a hemi-cane and uses a motorized wheelchair for long distances due to right-sided hemiparesis following a cerebrovascular accident (CVA), which causes generalized muscle weakness, poor endurance, unsteady gait, and poor balance. The patient fatigues easily and experiences dyspnea with moderate exertion, making any attempt to leave the home a considerable and taxing effort. Additionally, the patient requires assistance with all activities of daily living, including grooming, bathing, dressing, transferring, toileting, and managing medications. The patient's home environment also presents safety concerns, such as the presence of rugs in hallways, lack of grab bars in the bathroom, and poor lighting, which increase the risk of falls and injury if the patient attempts to leave the home independently. The patient has a history of urinary incontinence and contractures affecting the right arm and hand, further contributing to functional limitations. Allowable absences from the home are limited to medically necessary appointments, as outpatient therapy is currently deferred pending evaluation of palpitations related to medication. Given these limitations and safety concerns, skilled nursing services are medically necessary to monitor vital signs, provide education on disease processes and medication management, assess for progression of disease, and reduce the risk of hospitalization and injury. Occupational therapy is also indicated to improve upper body strength, conditioning, and functional status to maximize the patient's independence and safety at home. These documented functional deficits and medical conditions clearly meet Medicare criteria for homebound status and justify the need for skilled home health services. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN. Robert Bryant, a 71-year-old male patient admitted to home health care because they have significant functional impairments and medical conditions that severely limit their ability to leave the home without assistance. The patient is considered homebound as the patient ambulates with a hemi-cane and uses a motorized wheelchair for long distances due to right-sided hemiparesis following a cerebrovascular accident (CVA), which causes generalized muscle weakness, poor endurance, unsteady gait, and poor balance. The patient fatigues easily and experiences dyspnea with moderate exertion, making any attempt to leave the home a considerable and taxing effort. Additionally, the patient requires assistance with all activities of daily living, including grooming, bathing, dressing, transferring, toileting, and managing medications. The patient's home environment also presents safety concerns, such as the presence of rugs in hallways, lack of grab bars in the bathroom, and poor lighting, which increase the risk of falls and injury if the patient attempts to leave the home independently. The patient has a history of urinary incontinence and contractures affecting the right arm and hand, further contributing to functional limitations. Allowable absences from the home are limited to medically necessary appointments, as outpatient therapy is currently deferred pending evaluation of palpitations related to medication. Given these limitations and safety concerns, skilled nursing services are medically necessary to monitor vital signs, provide education on disease processes and medication management, assess for progression of disease, and reduce the risk of hospitalization and injury. Occupational therapy is also indicated to improve upper body strength, conditioning, and functional status to maximize the patient's independence and safety at home. These documented functional deficits and medical conditions clearly meet Medicare criteria for homebound status and justify the need for skilled home health services. Frequency and Duration: HH SN frequency: 2 times a week for 1 week, 1 time a week for 3 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, wound monitoring and management, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education. HH PT frequency: For evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and fall prevention techniques training effective on week of 04/16/2026. HH oT frequency: For evaluation and treatment focused on improving activities of daily living (ADLs), fine and gross motor skills, cognitive-perceptual function, adaptive techniques, and caregiver education. Goals: 1. Patient will perform grooming, bathing, and dressing with only setup or supervision (no hands-on assistance) for at least 5 of 7 days within 4 weeks. 2. Patient will participate in a toileting schedule and pelvic floor/behavioral strategies with caregiver assistance to reduce urinary incontinence episodes by 50% from baseline within 4 weeks. 3. Patient will report average right shoulder pain ≤3/10 at rest and ≤5/10 with activity and prescribed non-pharmacologic measures (ice/positioning), with documented decrease in pain episodes on 5 of 7 days within 4 weeks. 4. Patient will adhere to prescribed bowel regimen and report a spontaneous bowel movement at least every 48-72 hours, with reduced reports of constipation within 4 weeks. 5. Patient or caregiver will, implement recommended home safety modifications (installation/use of grab bars, removal of loose rugs, improvement of lighting) and demonstrate safe navigation of primary living areas within 4 weeks. draftNarratives.label: Medical Necessity
draftNarratives.draft: : This visit with a FTF encounter with the patient in the home is not a visit of convenience. This home visit is necessary to prevent exacerbations of patient's ongoing medical condition. Patient is homebound, ambulates with hemicane, with poor endurance and balance, unsteady gait, generalized muscle weakness, fatigues easily, dyspnea with moderate exertion and consequently leaving home would require a considerable and taxing effort. It is safer for patient to be seen in their own environment in lieu of an office setting. Subjective Patient, Robert Bryant is an 71 year-old African American male, alert and oriented x4. He lives with roommates, in a 2 story home.
